grounding_note: Tax routing depends on which taxpayer (individual vs business), which stage (audit/collection vs filed Tax Court petition vs criminal referral), and which tax (income vs estate/gift). Users say 'the IRS is after me' without these facts, all of which they hold in the notices they've received.

scenarios.opening_query: The IRS says I owe $40,000 I don't have and the letters keep getting scarier.
scenarios.hidden_fact: I already got the statutory notice of deficiency — my window to petition the U.S. Tax Court closes in 30 days.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: I got the notice of deficiency — my Tax Court petition window closes in 30 days.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a deficiency notice has started the Tax Court clock
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's still at the audit stage — an examiner keeps asking for receipts I can't find.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: Still just the audit — an examiner wants receipts I can't find.
scenarios.vetting_note: The 90-day letter is a hard procedural gate between the two labels; users know which letter came.

scenarios.opening_query: I'm being audited and my accountant suddenly says I need a lawyer, not just him.
scenarios.hidden_fact: Two IRS special agents from the criminal division came to my door with badges — my accountant says that means a fraud investigation.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: IRS criminal-division special agents showed up at my door — that's why he says get a lawyer.
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: No agents — it's a correspondence audit of my home-office deductions, but the numbers are big.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: No agents — just a paper audit of big home-office deductions.
scenarios.vetting_note: Special-agent contact is THE signal of criminal referral; accountants really do say exactly this.

scenarios.opening_query: My taxes are a disaster because of my business and the state is threatening to levy my accounts.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Taxation > Personal Income || Taxation > Business & Corporate
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's the company's payroll trust-fund taxes — my S-corp fell behind on withholding deposits and they're assessing the business and me as the responsible officer.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's the company's payroll trust-fund taxes — the S-corp fell behind on deposits and they're coming after the business and me as the officer.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the liability is business payroll tax rather than personal income tax
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my personal return — the business income flowed through and I couldn't pay my own tax bill.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my personal return — the pass-through income I couldn't pay tax on.
scenarios.vetting_note: Trust-fund liability vs pass-through personal debt: same 'business taxes' phrasing, different labels.

scenarios.opening_query: We inherited money from my mother last year and now there's a tax problem nobody warned us about.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Taxation > Personal Income || Taxation > Estate & Gift
scenarios.hidden_fact: The IRS is disputing the estate's valuation of Mom's business and demanding more estate tax from us as beneficiaries.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: The IRS disputes the estate's valuation of Mom's business and wants more estate tax from the beneficiaries.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether the dispute concerns estate tax rather than income tax
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's my own return — I cashed out the inherited IRA wrong and got taxed with penalties I want contested.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's my own return — I cashed the inherited IRA out wrong and the penalties need contesting.
scenarios.vetting_note: Inheritance triggers both regimes; which tax is in dispute is the routing fact.

scenarios.opening_query: I need a tax attorney. The government garnished my paycheck without any warning at all.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Taxation > Personal Income || Administrative Law > General (State)
scenarios.hidden_fact: It's not the IRS — the state employment department says I owe back 'employer' payroll assessments from when I had a nanny, and their administrative process railroaded me.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: It's not the IRS — the state employment department assessed me as a household 'employer' over our old nanny and their process railroaded me.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: which agency issued the garnishment and under what process
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: It's an IRS wage levy on my income-tax debt from two bad years.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: It's an IRS wage levy on my back income taxes.
scenarios.vetting_note: Agency identity separates tax practice from state administrative appeal; both garnish.

scenarios.opening_query: My ex filed our taxes wrong for years and now the government wants it all from me.
scenarios.plausible_initial_labels: Taxation > Personal Income || Family Law > General (Divorce/Separation etc.)
scenarios.hidden_fact: Our divorce decree makes him responsible for those tax years — I need the decree enforced against him in family court alongside my innocent-spouse request.
scenarios.fact_as_answer: Our decree puts those tax years on him — I need it enforced in family court while my innocent-spouse request runs.
scenarios.relevant_question_topic: whether a divorce decree allocates the liability and needs enforcement
scenarios.counterfactual_hidden_fact: We were never divorced when he filed — no decree exists; it's purely my innocent-spouse case with the IRS.
scenarios.counterfactual_fact_as_answer: We weren't divorced yet — there's no decree, so it's just my innocent-spouse case with the IRS.
scenarios.vetting_note: Decree existence adds a family-court enforcement route on top of the tax remedy.
